fee_schedule_full: Alabama Workers' Compensation Prevailing Rate/Maximum Fee Schedule for Physicians (and parallel schedules for each service category)
fee_schedule_agency: Alabama Department of Labor, Workers' Compensation Division
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus 5% plus dispensing fee (brand: AWP×105% + $11.12; generic: AWP×105% + $14.44) per Administrative Code Rule 480-5-5-.21
settlement_approval_agency_full: Alabama Circuit Court (county with jurisdiction over the claim)
settlement_approval_quirk: Alabama requires all lump-sum settlements to be approved by a circuit court judge, who determines whether the agreement is fair and in the injured worker's best interest before it becomes binding under Ala. Code § 25-5-56.
quirk_1: Alabama calculates permanent partial disability on either a physical impairment basis or a vocational disability basis; if the worker returns to a wage equal to or greater than the pre-injury wage, the rating is capped at the physician-assigned physical impairment percentage; if not, vocational disability evidence is admissible, which can substantially increase the rating and therefore the settlement value used to calibrate a WCMSA allocation.
quirk_2: The Medical Services Board updates fee schedules annually each March 1, so the applicable rate table for any projected future service depends on the service date, and a WCMSA must reflect the schedule in effect at the time of report completion.
quirk_3: Alabama has no mandatory drug formulary for workers' compensation, so projected pharmacy costs in a WCMSA should be based on the current pharmaceutics fee schedule rates (AWP-anchored).

fee_schedule_short: Alaska Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: Official Alaska Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule, amended and effective January 1, 2025 (8 AAC 45)
fee_schedule_agency: Alaska Division of Workers' Compensation, Department of Labor and Workforce Development
pharmacy_basis: AWP minus 10% of original manufacturer NDC (or AWP minus 10% of lowest-cost therapeutic equivalent, whichever is lower); compounded topicals capped at AWP minus 10% prorated by ingredient plus dispensing fee; custom compounds capped at $600 subject to review
settlement_approval_agency_full: Alaska Workers' Compensation Board, Division of Workers' Compensation, DOLWD
settlement_approval_quirk: Stipulations and settlements must be approved by the Alaska Workers' Compensation Board to be effective; the Board reviews compliance with statutory requirements before signing off.
quirk_1: An injured worker may change treating clinicians once as a matter of right; a second change requires written approval from the workers' compensation insurer, so the treating clinician roster available to support a WCMSA may be constrained by insurer sign-off rules.
quirk_2: The 2025 fee schedule follows AMA CPT guidelines for service coding; medical expenses are generally covered for up to two years post-injury unless the Workers' Compensation Board orders extended coverage, creating a specific evidentiary threshold for proving long-term future treatment in a WCMSA.
quirk_3: Alaska's 2025 pharmacy benefits manager legislation added transparency requirements for formulary management and drug substitution, which may affect which drugs are dispensed and at what cost during the WCMSA allocation period.

fee_schedule_short: Arizona Physicians' and Pharmaceutical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: Arizona Physicians' and Pharmaceutical Fee Schedule (RBRVS-based, updated annually effective October 1 through April 30)
fee_schedule_agency: Industrial Commission of Arizona (ICA)
pharmacy_basis: AWP via Medi-Span (current edition as of dispense date); insurer may elect an alternative basis by October 1 of each year
settlement_approval_agency_full: Industrial Commission of Arizona
settlement_approval_quirk: All full-and-final settlements require ICA approval with signed agreement from the carrier, employer, and worker; once approved, the settlement bars any future reopening of the claim.
quirk_1: Arizona uses a carrier-initiated IME process under ICA rules; a claimant has only three days after receiving IME notice to file objections, and the ICA rarely issues protective orders against the examination.
quirk_2: Full-and-final settlement authority was added by SB 1322, so not every legacy claim is governed by that pathway; older claims may still resolve through the traditional stipulation process, which affects what future-medical allocation language is needed in the WCMSA.
quirk_3: The pharmaceutical fee schedule allows the insurer to elect an alternative to Medi-Span AWP pricing by filing written notice with the ICA by October 1 each year, so the reimbursement basis in the WCMSA projection period may differ from the default.

fee_schedule_full: Arkansas Workers' Compensation Commission Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: Arkansas Workers' Compensation Commission (AWCC), a division of the Arkansas Department of Labor and Licensing
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $5.13 dispensing fee per prescription (lesser of fee schedule or provider's usual charge; generic equivalent required when available; PriceAlert/First DataBank is primary AWP reference)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Arkansas Workers' Compensation Commission
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlement proceeds via joint petition filed by both parties; the Commission holds a hearing, takes testimony under oath from both sides, and approves only if the arrangement is in the best interest of both parties; a lump sum may also be approved by mail using Form L.
quirk_1: Arkansas uses the AMA Guides Fourth Edition (exclusive of pain sections and range-of-motion tests) for impairment ratings, which yields different rating values than states using the Sixth Edition; the WCMSA projection must account for the impairment basis that governed the underlying claim.
quirk_2: Preauthorization is required for all non-emergency hospitalizations and for outpatient services projected to exceed $1,000 in billed charges on a single date of service; the medical cost containment program (Rule 30) governs these requirements and shapes which future treatments are documentable.
quirk_3: The AWCC fee schedule is RBRVS-based with Arkansas-specific conversion factors set by the Commission, so reimbursable rates for projected future services follow AWCC conversion factors as the cost basis.

fee_schedule_full: California Official Medical Fee Schedule, promulgated under Labor Code Section 5307.1 (Title 8 CCR Sections 9789.10 et seq.)
fee_schedule_agency: California Division of Workers' Compensation (DWC), Department of Industrial Relations
pharmacy_basis: OMFS Pharmacy Fee Schedule; physician-dispensed drugs billed per OMFS; OTC medications limited to 120% of documented cost up to $250 above cost
settlement_approval_agency_full: California Workers' Compensation Appeals Board
settlement_approval_quirk: A workers' compensation administrative law judge reviews and approves Compromise and Release agreements; the insurer must pay within 30 days of the approval order.
quirk_1: California's UR/IMR (Utilization Review/Independent Medical Review) system gates all medical-necessity disputes through a mandatory administrative process before any appeal to the WCAB, which shapes what future treatment a clinician can demonstrate is provable and reimbursable.
quirk_2: Injured workers must treat within an approved Medical Provider Network (MPN) when one is established by the employer or insurer; treatment outside the MPN is generally not reimbursable, which directly bounds the clinician roster available to treat conditions projected in the WCMSA.
quirk_3: QME/AME evaluations (Qualified or Agreed Medical Examiner) determine disputed medical-legal opinions on causation and permanent impairment; the rating from that process feeds directly into the settlement value and the future-medical allocation in the WCMSA.

fee_schedule_full: Colorado Division of Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule, Rule 18 (7 CCR 1101-3 Rule 18)
fee_schedule_agency: Colorado Department of Labor and Employment, Division of Workers' Compensation
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $4.00 dispensing fee for standard prescriptions; compounded medications with three or fewer ingredients reimbursed at AWP minus 50% of each ingredient NDC; gabapentin restricted to pharmacy-only dispensing effective January 1 2025
settlement_approval_agency_full: Colorado Division of Workers' Compensation, Administrative Law Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: Full and final settlements must be approved by a DOWC Administrative Law Judge, and the injured worker must complete an advisement process (individual or video) confirming understanding of the settlement terms before the agreement becomes binding.
quirk_1: Colorado is the only jurisdiction in the country that uses the AMA Guides to the Evaluation of Permanent Impairment, Third Edition Revised, to assign permanent partial disability ratings; a clinician preparing a WCMSA must project future care costs using the same edition-specific rating framework that anchors the settlement value.
quirk_2: When MMI or impairment rating is disputed, either party may trigger a Division Independent Medical Examination (DIME); the DIME physician's findings carry presumptive weight and are overturnable only by clear and convincing evidence, so the DIME outcome materially shapes the post-MMI treatment horizon the WCMSA must address.
quirk_3: Rule 16 Utilization Standards require prior authorization for any treatment that falls outside the Rule 17 Medical Treatment Guidelines; a clinician writing a WCMSA must account for whether projected future treatment is guideline-consistent or will require ongoing authorization, since non-authorized treatment will not be paid by the carrier after settlement.

fee_schedule_full: Official Connecticut Workers' Compensation Practitioner Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: Connecticut Workers' Compensation Commission (WCC)
pharmacy_basis: AWP of original NDC minus 10%, or AWP of lowest-cost therapeutic equivalent minus 10%, whichever is lower
settlement_approval_agency_full: Connecticut Workers' Compensation Commission
settlement_approval_quirk: Full and final settlements (called stipulations) require a formal stipulation approval hearing before a commissioner, who must find the agreement conforms to the Act; neither party is bound until the commissioner signs off.
quirk_1: If an employer has filed a certified Medical Care Plan (MCP) with the WCC, the injured worker's treating clinician must come from the MCP network for covered specialties; treatment outside that network is not compensable, which controls the pool of clinicians whose records will form the basis for a WCMSA projection.
quirk_2: Connecticut does not mandate a specific edition of the AMA Guides by statute, so permanency ratings default to the most current edition available; the edition applied at the time of a rating can affect the impairment percentage and, downstream, the scope of projected permanent care in a WCMSA.
quirk_3: WCC commissioners have stopped automatic administrative approval of Forms 36 (notices to reduce or discontinue benefits), so disputes over ongoing treatment can remain open longer and must be resolved before a settlement projection reflects the actual stabilized treatment plan.

fee_schedule_full: Delaware Workers' Compensation Health Care Payment System (HCPS) Fee Schedule (covering physicians, qualified health care professionals, hospitals, hospital outpatient facilities, and ambulatory surgery centers)
fee_schedule_agency: Delaware Department of Labor, Division of Industrial Affairs, Office of Workers' Compensation
pharmacy_basis: NADAC (or WAC if NADAC unavailable) plus $10 dispensing fee; physician-dispensed drugs carry no additional dispensing fee
settlement_approval_agency_full: Delaware Industrial Accident Board
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump-sum commutations require Industrial Accident Board approval, and the Board may impose a 5 percent reduction on the amount due as part of the commutation order.
quirk_1: Delaware prohibits use of the term "Independent Medical Examination" or "IME" in any workers' compensation proceeding or document; clinician-performed employer-requested exams carry a different statutory label and fines apply for violations, which affects how examination opinions are framed in a WCMSA narrative.
quirk_2: The HCPS is an RBRVS- and APC-based system with Delaware-specific rules developed separately from the Medicare payment system; rates are not a straight Medicare multiplier, so WCMSA pricing must use HCPS conversion factors as the cost basis.
quirk_3: Pharmacy reimbursement shifted to a NADAC-based methodology following 2021 legislation; WCMSAs projecting pharmaceutical costs must use NADAC-based pricing for drugs dispensed under the Delaware system.

fee_schedule_full: District of Columbia Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (set at 113 percent of Medicare reimbursement amounts)
fee_schedule_agency: District of Columbia Department of Employment Services, Office of Workers' Compensation
pharmacy_basis: VERIFY — DC's medical fee schedule is indexed at 113% of Medicare; no separately published WC pharmacy formulary or AWP-based rule confirmed
settlement_approval_agency_full: District of Columbia Department of Employment Services, Office of Workers' Compensation
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump-sum settlements require Office of Workers' Compensation approval based on findings that a valid dispute exists regarding compensability or the extent of disability; a stipulation alone does not close any aspect of the claim.
quirk_1: DC workers' compensation law is rooted in the federal Longshoremen's and Harbor Workers' Compensation Act framework, which DC adopted and then modified through its own statute (D.C. Code Title 32, Chapter 15); this lineage can create ambiguity about which procedural rules govern, and a WCMSA narrative should be explicit about the applicable DC statutory basis.
quirk_2: The medical fee schedule is directly pegged to Medicare at 113 percent and updated as Medicare updates; because the fee schedule tracks Medicare without a state-specific conversion factor, WCMSA pricing for fee-scheduled services can reference current Medicare rates adjusted by the 1.13 multiplier.
quirk_3: Permanent total disability benefits in DC continue for the life of the worker; for cases involving PTD, the WCMSA projection horizon is life expectancy and the open-ended medical obligation must be reflected fully in the allocation.

fee_schedule_full: Florida Workers' Compensation Health Care Provider Reimbursement Manual (Rule 69L-7.020, F.A.C.)
fee_schedule_agency: Florida Department of Financial Services, Division of Workers' Compensation
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $4.18 dispensing fee (retail pharmacy); physician-dispensed repackaged drugs capped at 112.5% of AWP plus $8.00 dispensing fee; compounded drugs require prior carrier authorization (2024 edition effective January 1 2025)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Florida Office of Judges of Compensation Claims, Division of Administrative Hearings
settlement_approval_quirk: For unrepresented claimants, the Judge of Compensation Claims must affirmatively find that a lump-sum settlement is in the claimant's best interest; for represented claimants, JCC review is required only to approve attorney fees, so many represented settlements proceed without a substantive judicial fairness review.
quirk_1: Florida statute limits temporary total and temporary partial disability benefits to a combined 104-week maximum, creating a statutory presumption that maximum medical improvement is reached by that point; the MMI date anchors the projection window for post-MMI future medical care in a WCMSA, and cases with long pre-MMI treatment periods may reach MMI by operation of the statute even if the treating clinician has not formally assigned it.
quirk_2: When the authorized treating clinician and a carrier's independent medical examiner disagree on a medical question, either party may request appointment of an Expert Medical Advisor (EMA) from the Division's certified roster; the EMA's opinion carries a presumption of correctness that can only be rebutted by clear and convincing evidence, making EMA conclusions a significant anchor for projected treatment needs.
quirk_3: Florida reimbursement is set at 175% of Medicare allowance for physician services and 210% for surgical procedures; a WCMSA written to Florida fee schedule rates will reflect materially higher projected costs than a CMS-priced allocation if CMS applies Medicare rates.

fee_schedule_short: Georgia Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: Georgia Workers' Compensation Medical and Dental Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: State Board of Workers' Compensation of Georgia
pharmacy_basis: AWP (per Medi-Span) plus per-NDC-per-day dispensing fee; as of April 1 2024, dispensing fees are $4.83 for brand-name and $7.25 for generic medications; topical compound medications capped separately per a May 2024 Board update
settlement_approval_agency_full: Georgia State Board of Workers' Compensation, Settlement Division
settlement_approval_quirk: All settlements must be submitted to and approved by the SBWC Settlement Division through documentary review under O.C.G.A. § 34-9-15 and Board Rule 15; once approved, the settlement is final and the Board may not subsequently modify or amend it.
quirk_1: Georgia employers must maintain either a traditional panel of at least six non-associated physicians (including one orthopedist) or a Board-certified Workers' Compensation Managed Care Organization (MCO); the authorized treating clinician within that panel or MCO controls the medical record that forms the basis for WCMSA projections, and any care obtained outside that structure is generally the claimant's expense.
quirk_2: Permanent impairment ratings in Georgia are governed exclusively by the AMA Guides, Fifth Edition, published in 2000; projections of future medical care in a WCMSA should be calibrated against diagnoses and functional findings documented under Fifth Edition methodology, since a clinician unfamiliar with Fifth Edition rating conventions may produce a report that conflicts with the controlling medical record.
quirk_3: Georgia's medical fee schedule is purchased from FAIR Health and updated on an April 1 cycle; the schedule covering the current year should be confirmed as of the report date, since rates applicable at time of service govern reimbursement.

fee_schedule_short: Hawaii Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: Hawaii Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule, Hawaii Administrative Rules Title 12 Chapter 15 (current exhibit effective January 1 2026)
fee_schedule_agency: Hawaii Department of Labor and Industrial Relations, Disability Compensation Division (DLIR/DCD)
pharmacy_basis: AWP-based; generic drugs reimbursed at AWP × 1.40 of original manufacturer NDC; compounded drugs at schedule rates by gram weight of ingredients; AWP fallback to WAC × 1.20 if AWP is unavailable
settlement_approval_agency_full: Hawaii Department of Labor and Industrial Relations, Disability Compensation Division (settlements reviewed and approved by the Labor and Industrial Relations Appeals Board, LIRAB)
settlement_approval_quirk: After all parties sign the settlement agreement, it is submitted to LIRAB for approval; a director-level and Deputy Attorney General review may also occur before LIRAB signs and files the agreement, after which a party has 31 days to make payments.
quirk_1: The injured worker may select any clinician practicing on the island where the injury occurred; if a specialist not available on that island is needed, the director may authorize treatment elsewhere, which can complicate projecting the clinician roster in a WCMSA for remote-island claimants.
quirk_2: Under HRS 386-79, the employer may request no more than one IME per claim unless good cause exists; the director may also appoint an impartial clinician paid from appropriated funds, adding a third potential medical opinion layer that can affect treatment projections.
quirk_3: Hawaii requires a hearing officer decision within 60 days of a hearing, and the Director of DLIR has authority to appoint an impartial clinician to resolve disputes, which can directly affect the medical evidence record that underlies a WCMSA allocation.

fee_schedule_full: Idaho Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (IDAPA 17.01.01.803, adopting CMS RBRVS)
fee_schedule_agency: Idaho Industrial Commission (IIC)
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus a dispensing fee for prescription medications; reasonable charge plus dispensing fee for OTC medications (per IDAPA 17.01.01.803)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Idaho Industrial Commission
settlement_approval_quirk: Effective July 1 2022, most Settlement Agreements must be filed with the IIC to be effective but no longer require Commission approval; lump-sum settlements remain subject to IIC approval and must be in the best interest of all parties.
quirk_1: Idaho allows employers to designate a treating clinician for all non-emergency work-related conditions; if a designated clinician is in place, the injured worker must obtain employer approval or IIC authorization before switching treaters, which bounds the clinician pool available for ongoing care projected in a WCMSA.
quirk_2: Idaho law defines "physician" broadly to include MDs, osteopaths, dentists, optometrists, podiatrists, chiropractors, and other state-licensed healing professionals, so a WCMSA must account for the possibility that chiropractic or other non-MD care may be ongoing and reimbursable.
quirk_3: Medical providers must submit written reports within 14 days of each visit to the insurer; failure creates billing disputes that can surface in records review, and gaps in reporting may affect the clinical picture underlying the WCMSA allocation.

fee_schedule_full: Illinois Workers' Compensation Commission Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: Illinois Workers' Compensation Commission (IWCC)
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $4.18 dispensing fee (for prescriptions dispensed outside a licensed pharmacy); licensed pharmacy retail claims paid at usual and customary
settlement_approval_agency_full: Illinois Workers' Compensation Commission (arbitrator assigned to the claim)
settlement_approval_quirk: All settlement contracts must be electronically submitted via the IWCC's CompFile system, reviewed by an assigned arbitrator, and signed by all parties before the arbitrator approves or rejects the contract based on whether the terms are in the claimant's best interest.
quirk_1: The IWCC fee schedule is based on historical charge data from 2002 to 2004; current payment is set at 53.2% of historical charges adjusted annually by CPI, so WCMSA projections must use IWCC schedule amounts as the cost basis.
quirk_2: Illinois' two-physician rule (820 ILCS 305/8(a)) gives the injured worker two independent treatment selections and each selection's full referral chain, so the treating clinician writing the WCMSA narrative may differ from whoever performed the original IME.
quirk_3: Employers may establish a Department of Insurance-approved Preferred Provider Program (PPP), which constrains the employee's physician choices to the PPP network; if a PPP is in place, the treating clinicians whose care is being projected must be evaluated for network status.

fee_schedule_full: Indiana Workers' Compensation Board Medical Service Facility Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: Worker's Compensation Board of Indiana (WCB)
pharmacy_basis: Reasonable and necessary cost basis; no formal pharmacy fee schedule; repackaged drugs reimbursed at AWP of original manufacturer NDC; physician office dispensing limited to first seven days post-injury
settlement_approval_agency_full: Worker's Compensation Board of Indiana
settlement_approval_quirk: A Board member must approve all settlement agreements; no settlement is valid without that approval, and the Board may refuse any agreement that does not conform with the Act's statutory rights.
quirk_1: Indiana's fee schedule applies only to medical service facilities (hospitals, hospital-based facilities, and medical centers) at 200% of Medicare rates; professional services from clinicians are reimbursed at reasonable and customary rates with no statutory cap, which complicates fee-basis projections in a WCMSA.
quirk_2: PPI ratings in Indiana may use any edition of the AMA Guides that the evaluating clinician considers appropriate to the specific impairment, creating variability in rating methodologies that can affect settlement structure and the post-settlement medical allocation.
quirk_3: Effective July 1 2024, Indiana shifted from employer-directed to employee-directed physician selection; for claims with dates of injury before and after that date, the applicable care-direction rule differs and shapes which treating clinician's records anchor the WCMSA narrative.

fee_schedule_full: Iowa has no published workers' compensation medical fee schedule; charges are subject to approval by the Iowa Workers' Compensation Commissioner as reasonable and necessary under Iowa Code § 85.27
fee_schedule_agency: Iowa Division of Workers' Compensation, Department of Inspections, Appeals, and Licensing (DIAL)
pharmacy_basis: Usual and customary charge; no state WC pharmacy fee schedule; where no controlling schedule exists, providers bill U&C; Iowa has no specific compounded medication rules distinct from general WC
settlement_approval_agency_full: Iowa Workers' Compensation Commissioner, Department of Inspections, Appeals, and Licensing
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlements must be submitted in writing to the Commissioner for approval; the Commissioner approves only if there is substantial evidence supporting the terms and the employee knowingly and voluntarily waives hearing rights; if the employee is represented by counsel, approval is presumed unless the Commissioner finds grounds to deny.
quirk_1: Iowa's absence of a medical fee schedule means there is no fixed reimbursement rate for projected future care; WCMSA future cost projections must rely on provider-level usual and customary charges or Medicare rates as a proxy, and the methodology should be documented on the face of the report.
quirk_2: Iowa uses the AMA Guides Fifth Edition (the Commissioner's task force rejected the Sixth Edition in 2008); the Fifth Edition rating methodology governs impairment determinations underlying any settlement, and the Commissioner retains authority to correct physician misapplication of the Guides.
quirk_3: Iowa's Alternate Medical Care procedure under § 85.27 and Iowa Administrative Code 876-4.48 allows an injured worker to petition for substitute care on an expedited basis (decision within 10-14 working days); active AMC disputes can affect which treating clinician's records form the medical foundation for a WCMSA.

fee_schedule_full: Kansas Department of Labor Workers' Compensation Schedule of Medical Fees (updated biennially; current edition effective May 2024, adopted under K.A.R. 51-9-7)
fee_schedule_agency: Kansas Department of Labor, Division of Workers' Compensation
pharmacy_basis: Lesser of the provider's usual and customary charge or the amount allowed by the KDOL Schedule of Medical Fees (AWP-based per the schedule; exact AWP discount percentage VERIFY against current edition)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Kansas Department of Labor, Division of Workers' Compensation Administrative Law Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlement awards on written stipulation may be approved by the assigned ALJ within five days of receiving the signed stipulation, provided both parties are represented and the claimant is over 18; otherwise a hearing is required.
quirk_1: Kansas uses the Official Disability Guidelines (ODG) from the Work Loss Data Institute as the statutory standard for evidence-based treatment; projected future treatment that deviates from ODG requires documented clinical justification, which shapes what a WCMSA can supportably include.
quirk_2: The KDOL fee schedule is updated on a biennial cycle, so reimbursement rates applicable to any given settlement may reflect a schedule that is up to two years old; the effective date of the applicable schedule should be confirmed at the time of WCMSA preparation.
quirk_3: Kansas permits claimants to self-administer their WCMSA and the KDOL has published a self-administration toolkit; this does not change the report preparation obligation but signals that the state administrative environment is familiar with the WCMSA construct.

fee_schedule_short: Kentucky WC Schedule of Fees for Physicians
fee_schedule_full: Kentucky Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule for Physicians (803 KAR 25:089)
fee_schedule_agency: Kentucky Department of Workers' Claims, Education and Labor Cabinet
pharmacy_basis: ODG by MCG Health pharmaceutical formulary (adopted 803 KAR 25:270, effective July 1 2019); Y-status drugs dispensed without preauthorization; N-status drugs require prior authorization; reimbursement for generics capped at the lesser of provider's usual and customary charge or 85% of AWP, plus a $5.00 dispensing fee
settlement_approval_agency_short: Department of Workers' Claims (Administrative Law Judge)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Kentucky Department of Workers' Claims, Administrative Law Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: All settlements require Administrative Law Judge approval via Form 110-I (Agreement as to Compensation and Order Approving Settlement); lump-sum commutation of future income benefits over $100 per week requires affirmative "reasonable assurance" of adequate income during disability, and future periodic payments are discounted to present value under KRS 342.265(3).
quirk_1: Kentucky mandates use of the AMA Guides, Fifth Edition for all permanent impairment ratings; a WCMSA report must align its diagnosis framing and impairment characterization with the controlling Fifth Edition methodology, because the ALJ weighs competing ratings against that standard when determining compensable conditions.
quirk_2: Kentucky adopted the ODG closed pharmaceutical formulary in 2019; projected pharmacy costs in a WCMSA must account for formulary status of each drug, since N-status drugs require documented prior authorization and may face future coverage challenges that affect the projected prescription cost line.
quirk_3: Kentucky's medical fee schedule is revised on a two-year cycle (July 1 of even-numbered years), with the current schedule obtained through FAIR Health; the applicable rate set for any projected future service date should be confirmed against the schedule effective at the time of report preparation.

fee_schedule_full: Louisiana Workers' Compensation Reimbursement Schedule (Louisiana Administrative Code, Title 40)
fee_schedule_agency: Louisiana Workforce Commission, Office of Workers' Compensation Administration (OWCA)
pharmacy_basis: AWP × 1.40 plus $11.81 dispensing fee per prescription (per Louisiana Title 40 Chapter 29; employer retains right to direct pharmacy choice)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Louisiana Office of Workers' Compensation Administration
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump sum or compromise settlement requires approval by a workers' compensation judge via Form LWC-WC-1011; if the worker is represented, an attorney affidavit may substitute for a hearing, but the judge retains authority to deny approval if the settlement is not fair and just.
quirk_1: Louisiana's OWCA operates nine district offices across the state, each with WC judges having original exclusive jurisdiction; the district where the injury occurred or the claimant resides controls venue, and district-level practice can vary in pace and local procedure.
quirk_2: Louisiana has its own Medical Treatment Guidelines (LAC Title 40, Chapters 20-23) for spine, pain, neurological, and extremity conditions; these guidelines govern what future treatment is approvable and shape which projected care is defensible in a WCMSA.
quirk_3: Preauthorization via Form LWC-WC-1010 is required once non-emergency treatment costs exceed $750; disputes over UR decisions go to the OWC Medical Director within 15 days, producing a binding recommendation that affects the medical record supporting any WCMSA projection.

fee_schedule_full: Medical Fee Schedule, Maine Workers' Compensation Board, 90-351 Chapter 5: Medical Fees; Reimbursement Levels; Reporting Requirements
fee_schedule_agency: Maine Workers' Compensation Board (WCB)
pharmacy_basis: Usual and customary charge; generic substitution required when available; no AWP-based formula in Chapter 5
settlement_approval_agency_full: Maine Workers' Compensation Board
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump-sum settlements require Board approval under 39-A M.R.S. §352; hearing officers must obtain a permanent impairment rating or a no-permanent-injury finding (on Form WCB-10) before approving any release of future medical liability.
quirk_1: Maine's WCB has adopted a rule (Chapter 12) requiring hearing officers to make a specific determination of expected future medical costs before approving any lump-sum settlement that releases medical liability; CMS has recognized these on-the-merits determinations, so a properly litigated Maine settlement can support the MSA figure without a separate CMS submission.
quirk_2: The Chapter 5 fee schedule ties professional service rates to Medicare's RBRVS methodology, so WCMSA projections should use the Medicare fee schedule as a pricing benchmark; Maine WCB updates the schedule twice yearly (January and October), and the applicable rate on the settlement date may differ from the rate used in the life-care projection.
quirk_3: Maine's Chapter 5 rules prohibit outpatient providers (other than licensed pharmacies) from receiving reimbursement for Schedule II-V controlled substances beyond an initial five-day supply; projected opioid or controlled-substance use in a WCMSA must account for this channel restriction.

fee_schedule_full: Maryland Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Guide (codified at COMAR 14.09.08)
fee_schedule_agency: Maryland Workers' Compensation Commission
pharmacy_basis: VERIFY — Maryland has no enacted WC-specific pharmacy fee schedule as of mid-2026; reimbursement has been set by insurers using AWP-based PBM contracts; SB 306 (2025 session) proposed an acquisition-cost-based schedule with target effective date September 1 2026 — confirm enacted status
settlement_approval_agency_full: Maryland Workers' Compensation Commission
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlements require WCC Commissioner approval through review of submitted medical records and the agreement itself; the Commission issues an Award Order upon finding that the settlement adequately protects the injured worker's interests, and the insurer has 15 days after approval to tender payment.
quirk_1: Maryland recognizes two distinct claim types (accidental personal injury and occupational disease) with separate filing deadlines and procedural tracks; a WCMSA covering occupational-disease claims must clearly identify the disease basis and the corresponding open medical obligations, which can differ from injury-based projections.
quirk_2: Maryland WCC settlements can take the form of a stipulation (keeping the award open) or a final compromise and settlement (closing all or part of the case); only the final compromise closes future medical liability, so the WCMSA should identify which settlement form is being used.
quirk_3: Maryland has no enacted pharmacy fee schedule as of the research date; projected pharmaceutical costs in a WCMSA must be based on current AWP or market-rate evidence because no statutory reimbursement ceiling for drugs applies, making the pharmacy allocation potentially higher than in states with capped schedules.

fee_schedule_full: Rates for Services under M.G.L. c. 152, Workers' Compensation Act (114.3 CMR 40.00)
fee_schedule_agency: Executive Office of Health and Human Services (EOHHS)
pharmacy_basis: Medicaid pharmacy fee schedule basis; 114.3 CMR 40.00 references the EOHHS prescribed-drugs regulation 101 CMR 331.00 governing reimbursement for both Medicaid and workers' compensation prescriptions
settlement_approval_agency_full: Department of Industrial Accidents
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump-sum settlements require approval at a Lump Sum Conference before a DIA administrative judge, who must find the agreement is in the employee's best interest; the judge questions the claimant on the record and the employer must file a Form 116A consent before approval is granted.
quirk_1: The professional fee schedule has not been updated since April 2009; the rates embedded in a WCMSA projection will therefore reflect rates well below current market charges, and a clinician preparing the projection must use fee-schedule rates for Massachusetts-based treatment regardless of actual billed amounts.
quirk_2: EOHHS has statutory authority to set the fee schedule while the DIA administers the claim; this split creates a governance gap where rate update proceedings run through EOHHS's separate rulemaking process even though the DIA hears the case.
quirk_3: Massachusetts uses the same pharmacy pricing regulation for workers' compensation and Medicaid, so drug reimbursement in a WCMSA projection must reference the current 101 CMR 331.00 Medicaid drug rate.

fee_schedule_full: Michigan Workers' Disability Compensation Agency Health Care Services Rules and Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: Michigan Workers' Disability Compensation Agency (WDCA), Michigan Department of Labor and Economic Opportunity (LEO)
pharmacy_basis: AWP minus 10% (per Red Book or Medi-Span) plus dispensing fee of $3.50 for brand-name drugs and $5.50 for generic drugs
settlement_approval_agency_full: Michigan Workers' Disability Compensation Agency Board of Magistrates
settlement_approval_quirk: All lump-sum settlements (called Redemptions of Liability) require approval at a hearing before a magistrate, who reviews the facts and medical records and must determine the amount is appropriate; a 15-day appeal window follows approval, which either party may waive.
quirk_1: Michigan is a wage-loss state, meaning weekly benefits are tied to actual diminished earning capacity; a WCMSA must account for the fact that settlement amounts are often calculated around wage-loss exposure, and the future medical component in the redemption order is what CMS uses to assess the MSA allocation.
quirk_2: Michigan's coordination-of-benefits statute (MCL 418.354) allows certain employer-paid benefits (social security, pension payments) to offset workers' comp wage-loss obligations, which affects the overall settlement structure and the proportion of settlement proceeds attributable to future medical care.
quirk_3: WDCA's Health Care Services Rules establish seven distinct Maximum Allowable Payment (MAP) calculation methods; the applicable MAP method depends on service type and provider category, so projecting future care costs requires identifying the correct MAP tier for each projected treatment modality.

fee_schedule_full: Minnesota Workers' Compensation Resource-Based Relative Value Fee Schedule (Minnesota Rules Chapter 5221)
fee_schedule_agency: Minnesota Department of Labor and Industry (DLI)
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $5.14 dispensing fee per prescription (standard outpatient); large hospital pharmacy dispensing reimbursed at AWP minus 12% plus $3.65 dispensing fee; payer liability is the lower of AWP-based amount or pharmacy's usual and customary charge (Minn. R. 5221.4070)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Minnesota Office of Administrative Hearings, Workers' Compensation Division — Compensation Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: A Compensation Judge must approve all Stipulations for Settlement, confirming the terms are fair, reasonable, and conforming with Chapter 176; a stipulation must be filed within 45 days of the parties reaching agreement, and approval is required before any settlement payment is final.
quirk_1: Minnesota's RBRVS schedule uses DLI-set conversion factors (updated annually each October 1); WCMSA cost projections should use current Minnesota-specific rates, which differ from Medicare rates for the same CPT codes.
quirk_2: Minnesota DLI maintains a separate Hospital Outpatient Fee Schedule (HOFS) alongside the RBRVS schedule; facility and professional fees are governed by different schedules, and projecting future care for claimants likely to use hospital-based outpatient services requires applying HOFS rates to those visits.
quirk_3: Minnesota's alternative dispute resolution process through DLI includes administrative conferences that can resolve contested medical and rehabilitation disputes before formal litigation; the outcome of those proceedings shapes which treatments are accepted as compensable, a factor that directly affects what future care is allocable in a WCMSA.

fee_schedule_full: Mississippi Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (Mississippi Administrative Code Title 20, Part 2)
fee_schedule_agency: Mississippi Workers' Compensation Commission
pharmacy_basis: AWP (as published) per ingredient plus a single $5.00 dispensing fee per day per NDC; compound topical medications capped at $300 per month for a maximum of 120 grams; billing must include original manufacturer NDC
settlement_approval_agency_full: Mississippi Workers' Compensation Commission, Administrative Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlement agreements must be approved by a Commission Administrative Judge, with the resulting order carrying the same force and effect as any other Commission decision; settlements involving minor claimants require prior Chancery Court authorization before Commission approval may be sought.
quirk_1: Mississippi's physician-selection rule gives the employer initial control over the treating clinician; the injured worker may then elect one physician of their own choice, and that physician may make one specialist referral per subspecialty; any further referrals require carrier approval; the WCMSA must be built on the treatment record of the controlling authorized treating clinician.
quirk_2: The MWCC medical fee schedule is based on Medicare Relative Value Units effective as of the 2019 adoption, administered through FAIR Health with periodic updates; the applicable conversion factor and RVU values in effect at the time of report preparation govern rate projections, and the schedule must be purchased from FAIR Health rather than downloaded from the Commission website.
quirk_3: Mississippi has no mandatory closed formulary for workers' compensation pharmacy, so WCMSA drug projections are governed by medical necessity and the AWP-plus-dispensing-fee cap.

fee_schedule_full: Missouri has no published workers' compensation medical fee schedule; all charges must be fair and reasonable and may not exceed the provider's usual and customary charge to private-pay or private-insurance patients (Missouri Revised Statutes § 287.140)
fee_schedule_agency: Missouri Department of Labor and Industrial Relations, Division of Workers' Compensation (DWC)
pharmacy_basis: Usual and customary charge; no state WC pharmacy fee schedule; Missouri § 287.140 requires charges not exceed the provider's U&C rate for non-WC patients; no AWP formula or formulary
settlement_approval_agency_full: Missouri Division of Workers' Compensation Administrative Law Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: An ALJ must approve any settlement agreement; the standard is that the settlement is free of fraud or undue influence, the employee understands and voluntarily accepts the terms, and the agreement is within a reasonable range of value; the ALJ does not conduct an independent deep review of medical records.
quirk_1: Missouri's active Second Injury Fund (SIF) compensates workers whose current injury combines with a pre-existing disability to produce greater combined disability; when a SIF claim is part of a settlement, future medical responsibility may be split between the employer's carrier and the Fund, and the WCMSA allocation must account for which entity is liable for which future care.
quirk_2: Missouri has no medical fee schedule, so WCMSA cost projections must be grounded in provider-level U&C charges or Medicare rates as a surrogate, with explicit documentation of the methodology used.
quirk_3: Medical fee disputes are resolved through an administrative process under 8 CSR 50-2.030; where a payer reduces a charge, the provider must submit an affidavit that fees are fair and reasonable, and the dispute process creates a record that may be relevant to contested future-care projections.

fee_schedule_full: Montana Workers' Compensation Professional Fee Schedule and Montana Workers' Compensation Facility Fee Schedule (Administrative Rule 24.29.1534)
fee_schedule_agency: Montana Department of Labor and Industry, Employment Relations Division
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $5.00 dispensing fee for brand drugs; AWP plus $15.22 dispensing fee for generics; reimbursement capped at lesser of provider's usual and customary charge negotiated contract amount or fee schedule; reimbursement keyed to original labeler NDC
settlement_approval_agency_full: Montana Department of Labor and Industry, Employment Relations Division
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlements and lump-sum conversions are subject to Employment Relations Division approval under MCA 39-71-741; the parties must first complete mandatory mediation through the DLI before any dispute may proceed to the Workers' Compensation Court, and the Court may review a departmental disapproval on petition.
quirk_1: Montana requires insurers to certify with the state, and a certified Managed Care Organization (MCO) may be contracted to designate the treating clinician; once an injured worker enters an MCO, the worker cannot change clinicians without insurer approval, so the MCO-designated treating record is the primary source for future-care projections in a WCMSA.
quirk_2: The Montana Utilization and Treatment Guidelines (montanaguidelines.com) establish evidence-based treatment benchmarks; treatment falling within the guidelines does not require prior authorization, but anything outside requires authorization per ARM 24.29.1593, and a WCMSA must reflect whether projected future treatment is guideline-conforming.
quirk_3: Montana maintains a standalone Workers' Compensation Court (a court of record separate from the district court system) that reviews benefit disputes after mediation has failed; settlement projections must account for the possibility that the Court is the final arbiter of contested medical necessity questions.

fee_schedule_short: Nebraska WC Court Medical Services Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: Nebraska Workers' Compensation Court Schedule of Fees for Medical Services (established under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 48-120; updated annually; current edition effective January 1 2025)
fee_schedule_agency: Nebraska Workers' Compensation Court
pharmacy_basis: AWP-based per the Court's published fee schedule (pharmacy reimbursement is included in the schedule adopted under § 48-120; no separate closed formulary; specific AWP discount or formula VERIFY directly from current schedule)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Nebraska Workers' Compensation Court
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump sum settlement applications must be filed with the Court under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 48-139 and Court Rule 47; the application requires a signed and verified petition from all parties, supporting medical reports, and a proposed approval order; a compromise settlement requires a showing of genuine dispute; payment must occur within 30 days of approval.
quirk_1: Nebraska workers' compensation is adjudicated by a specialized court (the Nebraska Workers' Compensation Court); single-judge panels handle initial hearings statewide, and appeals go directly to the Nebraska Court of Appeals.
quirk_2: The Nebraska WC Court fee schedule is reviewed at least biennially and updated annually effective January 1; it incorporates CMS RBRVS relative value units with Nebraska-specific conversion factors, so projected future costs follow the Court's schedule as the cost basis.
quirk_3: The Court's Legal section reviews every lump sum settlement application for adequacy and statutory compliance before a judge approves it, adding a substantive administrative review step that is separate from the judge's formal approval; incomplete applications are returned before the judge ever acts.

fee_schedule_full: Nevada Medical Fee Schedule, reviewed and revised annually by the Administrator of the Division of Industrial Relations, effective February 1 each year
fee_schedule_agency: Nevada Division of Industrial Relations (DIR), Department of Business and Industry
pharmacy_basis: AWP-based pending transition; Nevada adopted the ODG Drug Formulary (SB 317, signed June 2025) effective September 9 2025; full closed-formulary enforcement begins July 1 2027, with non-formulary coverage continuing until January 1 2028
settlement_approval_agency_full: Nevada Division of Industrial Relations
settlement_approval_quirk: Under NRS 616C.415, DIR must receive and approve stipulated settlement agreements; insurers must provide workers a written explanation of all settlement alternatives before any agreement is finalized.
quirk_1: Nevada's newly adopted ODG Drug Formulary (SB 317, 2025) will restrict reimbursable medications for all workers' compensation claims; any WCMSA projecting drug costs beyond 2027 must account for formulary status of each projected drug, since non-formulary drugs will be denied reimbursement absent a successful appeal to the Hearings Division.
quirk_2: For permanent partial disability ratings at or below 30% of whole person impairment, the full award may be paid as a lump sum; above 30%, only up to 30% may be paid as a lump sum with the remainder in installments, which constrains settlement structure and affects when future medical obligations are transferred.
quirk_3: Nevada uses designated treating clinician panels (provider panels) with rules updated alongside the SB 317 formulary legislation; if a claimant is directed to treat within a panel, projected future treating clinicians in the WCMSA must reflect that panel structure.

fee_schedule_full: New Hampshire does not publish a workers' compensation medical fee schedule
fee_schedule_agency: New Hampshire Department of Labor (Workers' Compensation Division)
pharmacy_basis: No WC-specific pharmacy fee schedule; pharmacy reimbursement governed by usual and customary charge under RSA 281-A:23
settlement_approval_agency_full: New Hampshire Department of Labor, Workers' Compensation Division
settlement_approval_quirk: All settlements where compensability has been admitted or ordered require Commissioner approval; a formal hearing is required before approval, and lump-sum settlements are generally approved only after twelve months of continuous disability.
quirk_1: The absence of a published fee schedule means WCMSA projections for New Hampshire claimants cannot use a state-specific maximum allowable rate; instead, projections must use the provider's usual and customary charge or, where a managed care network contract applies, the negotiated rate, requiring verification of network participation for each projected provider.
quirk_2: New Hampshire operates a two-tier medical payment structure: managed care networks negotiate fees for participating clinicians, while providers outside those networks bill usual and customary charges that carriers may challenge as excessive under RSA 281-A:24; the applicable tier for a given claimant must be identified when projecting future treatment costs.
quirk_3: Medical costs in New Hampshire workers' compensation account for a high share of total claim costs (above the national average), which reflects the absence of fee-schedule price controls; this cost environment makes accurate WCMSA pricing more variable and carrier scrutiny of projections higher.

fee_schedule_full: New Jersey workers' compensation medical services are reimbursed at reasonable and customary rates under N.J.S.A. 34:15-15
fee_schedule_agency: New Jersey Division of Workers' Compensation, Department of Labor and Workforce Development
pharmacy_basis: VERIFY (no WC-specific pharmacy fee schedule confirmed; likely usual and customary basis consistent with the absence of a medical fee schedule)
settlement_approval_agency_full: New Jersey Division of Workers' Compensation
settlement_approval_quirk: Settlements proceed as either a Section 20 dismissal (N.J.S.A. 34:15-20), which fully extinguishes future rights and requires a genuine disputed issue, or a Section 22 Order Approving Settlement, which assigns a disability percentage and preserves the right to reopen; a judge of compensation must approve both types.
quirk_1: The employer and carrier control the choice of authorized treating clinician under N.J.S.A. 34:15-15; treatment outside the authorized provider is not compensable except in emergencies, so the treating clinician roster that forms the WCMSA medical history is entirely employer-directed, which can affect the completeness and objectivity of the clinical record.
quirk_2: Because there is no published medical fee schedule, WCMSA projections for New Jersey claimants must use the provider's reasonable and customary charge; this can produce higher projected costs than in fee-schedule states and warrants explicit documentation of pricing methodology in the report.
quirk_3: Section 20 dismissals require a disputed issue of liability, causation, or jurisdiction; a WCMSA tied to a Section 20 settlement must clearly document the basis for dispute, because a settlement that looks like an accepted claim resolved for payment convenience may not qualify, affecting the legal posture of the MSA.

fee_schedule_short: WCA Health Care Provider Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: New Mexico Workers' Compensation Administration Health Care Provider Fee Schedule and Billing Instructions
fee_schedule_agency: New Mexico Workers' Compensation Administration (WCA)
pharmacy_basis: AWP of the lowest-priced therapeutically equivalent drug (per unit, prorated from the "100s each" quantity as listed in a nationally recognized publication on the date of dispensing); no separate closed formulary
settlement_approval_agency_full: New Mexico Workers' Compensation Administration
settlement_approval_quirk: Lump sum settlement requires a hearing before a workers' compensation judge; the judge must find the agreement fair, equitable, and in substantial compliance with the Act, and must ensure unrepresented workers affirmatively understand that medical benefits cease permanently upon approval (NMSA §§ 52-5-12 and 52-5-13).
quirk_1: New Mexico policy disfavors lump sum settlement; the statute (§ 52-5-12) states it is in the best interest of injured workers to receive periodic benefits, so judges scrutinize lump sum proposals more closely than in most states.
quirk_2: Mediation is mandatory before a formal hearing can proceed; the WCA mediator issues a Recommended Resolution that binds both parties if neither rejects it, so many cases resolve at mediation without a full trial record, affecting the depth of medical documentation available.
quirk_3: The WCA fee schedule is updated annually by Director's Order and is not RBRVS-identical to Medicare; conversion to projected future costs uses the current WCA schedule rates as the cost basis.

fee_schedule_short: Official NY WC Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: Official New York Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (incorporating the New York State Workers' Compensation Drug Formulary for pharmaceuticals)
fee_schedule_agency: New York State Workers' Compensation Board (WCB)
pharmacy_basis: Brand drugs: AWP minus 12% plus $4.00 dispensing fee; generic drugs: AWP minus 20% plus $5.00 dispensing fee (effective October 1 2019); subject to the state drug formulary's preferred/non-preferred/exempt classification
settlement_approval_agency_full: New York State Workers' Compensation Board
settlement_approval_quirk: Full and final settlements proceed under Workers' Compensation Law Section 32 as Waiver Agreements; the Board (or a designee) reviews the agreement administratively, issues provisional approval, allows a ten-day withdrawal period, then issues a formal decision; payment is due within ten days of the formal decision.
quirk_1: New York's Medical Treatment Guidelines (MTGs, mandatory since December 2010 and last updated May 2022) set evidence-based treatment standards for major body regions; treatment outside MTG parameters requires prior authorization through the Board's OnBoard system, and this authorization history shapes what future treatment is demonstrably probable for WCMSA purposes.
quirk_2: New York's drug formulary (effective March 2022) classifies drugs as Phase A, Phase B, perioperative, or non-formulary; non-formulary drugs require prior authorization from the carrier, and any controlled substance or specialty drug projected in a WCMSA must be checked against current formulary status because reimbursability depends on the formulary classification.
quirk_3: Schedule Loss of Use (SLU) awards compensate permanent functional loss of scheduled extremities and are distinct from permanency classification cases; an SLU case can settle without a finding of total or partial disability, and the medical picture underlying an SLU settlement (including remaining treatment need) must still be fully captured in the WCMSA even though the indemnity structure differs from a classification case.

fee_schedule_full: North Carolina Industrial Commission Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_agency: North Carolina Industrial Commission
pharmacy_basis: 95% of AWP per unit as of the date of dispensing (the lesser of that amount or the provider's contracted rate with the payor); original manufacturer NDC required on all billing; no closed formulary
settlement_approval_agency_full: North Carolina Industrial Commission (Deputy Commissioner)
settlement_approval_quirk: All compromise settlement agreements (called "clinchers") must be submitted to and approved by a Deputy Commissioner of the North Carolina Industrial Commission, who reviews for fairness and the best interest of all parties under NCIC Rule 502; the agreement must confirm that all medical expenses through the settlement date are addressed by the employer or carrier before approval is granted.
quirk_1: The NCIC fee schedule is anchored to 1995 North Carolina Medicare values multiplied by service-category-specific factors (for example, 1.58 for general medicine CPT codes, 2.05 for evaluation and management codes 99201-99215); because the base year is 1995, the resulting NCIC rate may be significantly higher or lower than current Medicare rates depending on the CPT code, and a WCMSA must use the applicable NCIC maximum.
quirk_2: Where neither the NCIC fee schedule nor a contracted rate applies, the reimbursement ceiling is the provider's usual, customary, and reasonable charge under G.S. § 97-26; this means some services for which the NCIC schedule is silent may be projected at UCR levels, and the WCMSA must identify which rate basis applies to each projected service.
quirk_3: North Carolina uses a Form 26A agreement to memorialize accepted claims and compensation amounts, and a separate compromise settlement agreement (clincher) to close future medical; a settlement that closes only indemnity while leaving medical open is procedurally distinct from a full clincher, and the WCMSA is only relevant to closures that include future medical.

fee_schedule_full: North Dakota Workforce Safety & Insurance Fee Schedule Guidelines (Medical Provider, Pharmacy, Inpatient Hospital, and DME schedules)
fee_schedule_agency: North Dakota Workforce Safety & Insurance (WSI)
pharmacy_basis: WAC plus 8% plus a per-item dispensing fee of $4.00 (or UCR whichever is lesser); pharmacy fee schedule based on Wolters Kluwer Medi-Span Electronic Drug File WAC data; WSI contracts with LucyRx as pharmacy benefit manager
settlement_approval_agency_full: North Dakota Workforce Safety & Insurance
settlement_approval_quirk: WSI is the sole insurer and the sole settlement authority in North Dakota's monopolistic system; there is no private insurer counterparty, so all settlement negotiations are conducted directly with WSI, which may separately close a disability claim while holding medical benefits open, or may convert benefits to a structured arrangement.
quirk_1: North Dakota's monopolistic fund structure means WSI simultaneously administers claims, sets reimbursement rates, and functions as the settlement counterparty; a WCMSA clinician must project future care costs against WSI's own fee schedule, because no private-insurer variation applies.
quirk_2: WSI uses ODG by MCG (formerly Official Disability Guidelines) as its evidence-based medical treatment guideline framework; projected future treatment that falls outside ODG benchmarks requires prior authorization, and the WCMSA must account for whether WSI's utilization review process would support projected treatment.
quirk_3: A claim is presumed closed if WSI has not paid any benefit for four years; this dormancy rule can affect how ongoing medical benefit entitlement is established in a WCMSA settlement, and active treatment records should be maintained to avoid inadvertent claim closure.

fee_schedule_full: Ohio Bureau of Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (administered under Ohio Administrative Code Chapter 4123-6)
fee_schedule_agency: Ohio Bureau of Workers' Compensation (BWC)
pharmacy_basis: AWP minus 15% plus $3.50 dispensing fee; 2025 proposed rule amendments would cap non-sterile compound reimbursement at $100 and replace fixed compound dispensing fees with time-based fees
settlement_approval_agency_full: Ohio Bureau of Workers' Compensation (BWC) with Industrial Commission of Ohio (IC) review
settlement_approval_quirk: State-fund settlements require BWC approval via Form C-240, followed by a 30-day IC review window during which the IC may disapprove only for clear unfairness; if the IC does not act, the settlement is approved by operation of law, after which a mandatory 30-day cooling-off period runs before finality.
quirk_1: Ohio's WC claims are allowed or denied on a condition-by-condition basis, and BWC's reimbursement is limited to the allowed conditions in the claim; a WCMSA must address only conditions that have been formally allowed, and any additional conditions requested via C-9 but not yet allowed are not includable until and unless BWC allows them.
quirk_2: Ohio BWC is the sole insurer for non-self-insured employers (monopolistic state-fund jurisdiction); private WC carriers do not operate in this market, so all state-fund claim settlement procedures, fee schedule rates, and formulary rules flow through BWC rather than commercial insurer programs.
quirk_3: Ohio BWC maintains a fee lookup tool (searchable by CPT code) and publishes separate fee schedules for physician services, hospital outpatient (OPPS-based), and ambulatory surgery centers; the applicable schedule depends on the setting of care, so projecting future treatment costs in a WCMSA requires matching each projected service to the correct Ohio BWC fee schedule tier.

fee_schedule_full: Oklahoma Workers' Compensation Commission Schedule of Medical and Hospital Fees
fee_schedule_agency: Oklahoma Workers' Compensation Commission (WCC)
pharmacy_basis: 90% of AWP plus $5.00 dispensing fee per prescription; closed formulary adopted by rule under Title 85A § 50; non-formulary drugs require preauthorization with a documented plan of care; generic equivalent required when available
settlement_approval_agency_full: Oklahoma Workers' Compensation Commission
settlement_approval_quirk: Joint petition settlements are filed with the WCC and approved by a Commissioner; a Benefit Review Officer who conducted the preliminary conference on a claim may not approve the joint petition for that same claim.
quirk_1: Oklahoma requires impairment ratings for permanent partial disability to use the AMA Guides Sixth Edition, which produces different rating values than states still using older editions; the Sixth Edition methodology is the baseline for understanding the disability scope in any WCMSA.
quirk_2: Oklahoma's closed formulary (adopted under 85A § 50, implemented via WCC rules and ODG) requires preauthorization for drugs not on the formulary; every drug projected in a WCMSA must be checked against formulary status, because non-formulary projection without a documented clinical exception is contestable.
quirk_3: ODG Treatment in Workers' Compensation is the statutory evidence-based treatment standard in Oklahoma (85A § 16); projected future treatment that falls outside ODG parameters requires additional clinical support to withstand scrutiny.
caselaw_citation: Vasquez v. Dillard's, Inc., 2016 OK 89, 381 P.3d 768

fee_schedule_full: Oregon Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule, Oregon Administrative Rules Chapter 436 Division 009
fee_schedule_agency: Oregon Workers' Compensation Division (WCD), Department of Consumer and Business Services (DCBS)
pharmacy_basis: AWP-based per OAR 436-009-0090; non-compounding pharmacy at a scheduled percentage of AWP plus dispensing fee; compounding pharmacy at 83.5% of AWP per ingredient plus $10 compounding and dispensing fee; insurer uses a nationally published pricing guide (RED BOOK or Medi-Span)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Oregon Workers' Compensation Board
settlement_approval_quirk: Claim Disposition Agreements (CDAs) on accepted claims are reviewed and approved by the Workers' Compensation Board; the Board may reject a CDA if no bona fide dispute exists or if the proposed disposition is not reasonable, and workers retain a 30-day cooling-off period to request disapproval (waivable if represented by counsel).
quirk_1: Under Oregon's CDA structure, a worker retains medical benefits for life even after a CDA closes indemnity; the WCMSA must therefore account for continuing medical obligations that the insurer still owes, distinguishing what is allocated to the WCMSA from what remains an open carrier obligation.
quirk_2: Oregon operates as a no-fault system with a 60-day window for insurers to accept or deny claims; new or omitted conditions also trigger a separate 60-day decision cycle, so the accepted-condition scope can shift post-settlement and affect whether WCMSA projections are complete.
quirk_3: Oregon's 2025 billing code mandate requires use of AMA CPT 2025, HCPCS 2025, and ADA CDT 2025 codes on all bills; a WCMSA projecting future treatment must use current-year coding to ensure projected services map to reimbursable code sets.

fee_schedule_full: Pennsylvania Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (Part A and Part B) administered under 34 Pa. Code Chapter 127
fee_schedule_agency: Pennsylvania Department of Labor and Industry, Bureau of Workers' Compensation, Health Care Services Review Division
pharmacy_basis: 110% of AWP per unit as of the date of dispensing (statutory basis under the PA Workers' Compensation Act); ongoing 2024-2025 litigation centers on whether NADAC or the Red Book AWP source applies; Bureau has moved toward NADAC with no dispensing fee added under that methodology
settlement_approval_agency_full: Pennsylvania Bureau of Workers' Compensation, Workers' Compensation Office of Adjudication — Workers' Compensation Judge
settlement_approval_quirk: All Compromise and Release Agreements (C&R) under Section 449 of the Act require a hearing before a Workers' Compensation Judge who must find on the record that the injured worker understands the terms; neither party is bound until the WCJ signs the approval order.
quirk_1: Pennsylvania's Impairment Rating Evaluation (IRE) process, triggered after 104 weeks of total disability compensation, can convert a claimant's status from total to partial disability (capping benefits at 500 weeks) if the whole-body impairment rating is below 35%; WCMSA projections must account for whether IRE status has changed the benefit trajectory at settlement.
quirk_2: Pharmacy reimbursement is statutorily set at 110% of AWP, but the Commonwealth Court has questioned which AWP publication controls; a WCMSA projecting drug costs in Pennsylvania should note this pricing ambiguity and use the current Bureau-applied methodology at the time of allocation.
quirk_3: Pennsylvania's utilization review (UR) process under 34 Pa. Code Chapter 127 Subchapter C allows any party to challenge the reasonableness or necessity of treatment; a UR denial that has become final limits what future treatment is provable for WCMSA purposes, and any UR-denied treatment category should be addressed explicitly in the allocation rationale.

fee_schedule_full: Rhode Island Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (issued annually by the Director of the Department of Labor and Training)
fee_schedule_agency: Rhode Island Department of Labor and Training (DLT)
pharmacy_basis: AWP up to 120%; if the pharmacy provides an invoice, reimbursement may be up to 120% of actual cost
settlement_approval_agency_full: Rhode Island Workers' Compensation Court
settlement_approval_quirk: All settlements must be approved by a judge of the Workers' Compensation Court under R.I. Gen. Laws §§28-33-25 and 28-33-25.1; the court distinguishes between denial-and-dismissal settlements (pre-liability) and commutation settlements (post-liability), and commutation petitions must address whether future medical needs are funded.
quirk_1: Rhode Island routes all workers' compensation disputes through a specialized judicial court (created in 1991); every contested element of a WCMSA-adjacent settlement, including medical allocation, is resolved through judicial proceedings before an appointed judge, which affects documentation standards and hearing timelines.
quirk_2: Injured workers retain the right to choose the first treating clinician; if the insurer has filed a Preferred Provider Network (PPN) with the Medical Advisory Board, any subsequent clinician change must be to a PPN-listed provider, making the treating clinician roster for WCMSA purposes dependent on which PPN, if any, applies to the claim.
quirk_3: Commutation petitions must disclose the Medicare set-aside amount to the court, so the WCMSA figure becomes part of the judicial record and is subject to the judge's review of whether the settlement adequately addresses future medical needs.

fee_schedule_full: South Carolina Workers' Compensation Commission Medical Services Provider Manual
fee_schedule_agency: South Carolina Workers' Compensation Commission, Medical Services Division
pharmacy_basis: AWP of the original manufacturer's NDC as of the date of dispensing (sourced from Medi-Span or IBM Micromedex RED BOOK), plus a $5.00 dispensing fee for repackaged or relabeled drugs; compound drugs require prior authorization per dispensing; if AWP data is unavailable, WAC plus 20% substitutes; professional services conversion factor set at $52.00 effective March 2026
settlement_approval_agency_full: South Carolina Workers' Compensation Commission (Commissioner)
settlement_approval_quirk: Workers' compensation settlements in South Carolina are approved by a Commissioner of the SCWCC; unrepresented claimants must attend a settlement conference with the Commissioner, who scrutinizes the agreement for fairness, while represented parties may proceed through a more streamlined filing process, with a $50 filing fee for all settlements.
quirk_1: South Carolina employers control the initial selection of the authorized treating clinician; the SCWCC has discretion to authorize care from an unauthorized clinician in certain circumstances, but the treating record built under the employer-selected clinician is the primary evidentiary basis for projecting future medical need in a WCMSA.
quirk_2: The authorized treating clinician completes a Form 14B (Physician's Statement) at or near MMI, documenting diagnosis, work restrictions, and whether future medical care will be required; that form is a primary source document for WCMSA projections, and a projection that departs materially from the Form 14B findings requires clear supporting documentation.
quirk_3: South Carolina's medical fee schedule conversion factor is set by Commission vote and updated periodically; the 2026 conversion factor of $52.00 for professional services was approved at the March 2026 business meeting, so projected future costs must use the rate in effect at the time of the report, confirmed against the current Manual edition.

fee_schedule_short: South Dakota WC Medical Fee Schedule
fee_schedule_full: South Dakota Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (Rule 47:03:05), based on Optum Relative Values for Physicians (RVP)
fee_schedule_agency: South Dakota Department of Labor and Regulation, Division of Labor and Management
pharmacy_basis: Usual and customary charge; South Dakota has no state-specific pharmacy fee schedule for workers' compensation and does not publish AWP-based reimbursement ceilings; no physician-dispensing guidance is published
settlement_approval_agency_short: SD Division of Labor and Management
settlement_approval_agency_full: South Dakota Department of Labor and Regulation, Division of Labor and Management
settlement_approval_quirk: Under SDCL 62-7-5, a settlement memorandum filed with the Division is deemed approved and enforceable if the Division does not object within twenty days; there is no judicial approval requirement, and the 20-day non-response window constitutes constructive approval.
quirk_1: Full and final settlements that close medical benefits are available in South Dakota only when compensability is genuinely at issue; if liability is accepted, medical benefits generally remain open, and a WCMSA must reflect that future injury-related treatment may remain a carrier obligation unless compensability itself is in dispute.
quirk_2: South Dakota adopted the 2025 Optum Relative Values for Physicians (RVP) effective late 2025, updating from the 2020 edition; because the fee schedule is RVP-based, a clinician projecting future medical costs should confirm which RVP edition is in effect at the time of the WCMSA to use the correct relative value units.
quirk_3: South Dakota is a worker-choice state for treating clinician selection; the worker notifies the employer before or as soon as practicable after treatment, and changes of clinician require written insurer permission, so the treating clinician record and any referrals are worker-controlled and directly shape the future-care record on which the WCMSA is built.

fee_schedule_full: Tennessee Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule (administered under Bureau of Workers' Compensation rules Chapter 0800-02-18)
fee_schedule_agency: Tennessee Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Bureau of Workers' Compensation
pharmacy_basis: AWP (current pricing as of date of service, updated per a 2025 rule amendment moving from monthly publication to current pricing); subject to ODG by MCG Health Drug Formulary (Chapter 0800-02-25); Y-status drugs dispensed without preauthorization; N-status drugs and all compounded, topical, and investigational drugs require prior approval; rate tables updated April 1 each year and obtained through FAIR Health
settlement_approval_agency_short: Bureau of Workers' Compensation, Court of Workers' Compensation Claims
settlement_approval_agency_full: Tennessee Bureau of Workers' Compensation, Court of Workers' Compensation Claims
settlement_approval_quirk: A workers' compensation judge must review and approve or reject all settlements within three business days of receipt; the judge determines whether the employee is receiving substantially the benefits provided by the Tennessee Workers' Compensation Law, and if the claimant is unrepresented, the judge is statutorily required to fully inform the claimant of available benefits and procedural rights before approving.
quirk_1: Tennessee's 2013 Workers' Compensation Reform Act (effective July 1 2014) transferred all claim jurisdiction from civil courts to an administrative Court of Workers' Compensation Claims; claims with injury dates before July 1 2014 remain in a separate pre-reform legal framework with different appeal paths, so the applicable procedural law governing a WCMSA settlement depends on the injury date.
quirk_2: Tennessee adopted the ODG pharmaceutical formulary as the state's mandatory drug formulary; N-status drugs in the ODG require preauthorization, and a WCMSA that projects N-status medications must document the carrier's prior approval or the clinician's plan to seek it, since post-settlement reimbursability of those drugs at Tennessee fee schedule rates is not automatic.
quirk_3: Tennessee Bureau rules explicitly state that the Bureau does not regulate WCMSAs and cannot assist with price disputes after medical benefits are closed; a WCMSA priced at Tennessee fee schedule rates will not be enforced by the Bureau after settlement, and the set-aside funds become the claimant's responsibility to administer against Medicare's requirements independently.

fee_schedule_full: Texas Division of Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Guidelines (28 TAC Chapter 134)
fee_schedule_agency: Texas Department of Insurance, Division of Workers' Compensation (DWC)
pharmacy_basis: AWP × 1.25 per unit plus $4.00 dispensing fee per prescription for generics; closed formulary based on ODG Appendix A; drugs with "N" (not recommended) status require preauthorization; generic substitution required when available
settlement_approval_agency_full: Texas Department of Insurance, Division of Workers' Compensation
settlement_approval_quirk: Disputes proceed through a Benefit Review Conference before an ALJ contested case hearing; settlement is executed on DWC Form-25 and must be signed by all parties and submitted to the DWC field office for approval.
quirk_1: Texas's closed pharmacy formulary, governed by 28 TAC § 134.530 and the ODG Appendix A "N" drug list, requires that every drug projected in a WCMSA be evaluated against current formulary status; N-status drugs require preauthorization documentation for the projection to be defensible.
quirk_2: Approximately 28-30% of Texas private employers opt out of the WC system as non-subscribers; non-subscriber injury claims are tort actions outside the DWC system, and WCMSAs apply only to settlements of claims covered by actual DWC workers' compensation coverage.
quirk_3: DWC conversion factors are adjusted annually based on the Medicare Economic Index; the 2025 office-setting surgery conversion factor is $70.18, and WCMSA projections for TX use DWC conversion factors as the cost basis.

fee_schedule_full: Utah Medical Fee Standard (Utah Admin. Code R612-300), adopted by the Utah Labor Commission with approval of the Workers' Compensation Advisory Council
fee_schedule_agency: Utah Labor Commission, Division of Industrial Accidents
pharmacy_basis: AWP for the underlying medication as identified by its original labeler NDC; if AWP ceases to be published substitute WAC plus 20%; physician dispensing is largely prohibited
settlement_approval_agency_full: Utah Labor Commission, Division of Adjudication, Administrative Law Judge
