settlement_approval_quirk: All settlements require Labor Commission approval through an ALJ; Utah ALJs routinely require a written explanation of how Medicare's interest is addressed before approving any full and final settlement, making WCMSA documentation a practical prerequisite to settlement approval even though Utah law does not independently mandate it.
quirk_1: Utah's fee standard is based on the CMS RBRVS with conversion factors set by the Workers' Compensation Advisory Council (WCAC); the WCAC-approved rate differs from the CMS Medicare rate, so a WCMSA that uses Medicare pricing as a proxy may understate or overstate allowable charges depending on the procedure.
quirk_2: Medical benefits are lifetime for injuries on or after May 1 2007; for older injuries (July 1 1988 through April 30 2007) the worker must have billed the carrier at least once every three years to preserve ongoing eligibility, so the benefit duration on the WCMSA horizon is injury-date dependent and should be confirmed at claim review.
quirk_3: Utah Senate Bill 190 (effective May 7 2025) prohibits health care providers from balance billing for workers' compensation claims and expands the Labor Commission's authority over provider fees; this constraint sets a ceiling on what providers may collect and limits the billing basis available for future-cost projection.

fee_schedule_full: Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedule Rule 40.000
fee_schedule_agency: Vermont Department of Labor
pharmacy_basis: AWP (per Red Book or equivalent) plus $3.15 dispensing fee capped at the provider's actual charge
settlement_approval_agency_full: Vermont Department of Labor, Workers' Compensation Division
settlement_approval_quirk: Compromise settlements (Form 16) require Commissioner approval; approval is granted only when the agreement is in the employee's best interest or when there is a genuine dispute, and the Division must review fund distribution before the settlement becomes binding.
quirk_1: A Form 22 Agreement for Permanent Partial Disability closes indemnity liability but leaves the medical claim open by operation of Vermont law; the employer and carrier remain obligated to pay for future work-related medical care even after a PPD settlement, so the WCMSA projection applies only to settlements that include a specific medical compromise under a separate Form 16.
quirk_2: Vermont's fee schedule (Rule 40) uses an RBRVS-adjacent methodology for professional services; WCMSA projections for Vermont claimants must be priced against Rule 40 rates, which are updated periodically by the Department of Labor and may not track Medicare fee schedule movements on the same schedule.
quirk_3: Vermont has no independent utilization review or treatment guideline framework equivalent to some larger states; treatment disputes go to the Commissioner through an expedited hearing process, and an unresolved treatment dispute at settlement can complicate defining the "reasonably probable future treatment" scope for a WCMSA.

fee_schedule_full: Virginia Workers' Compensation Medical Fee Schedules (biennial; current edition effective January 1 2026 through December 31 2027)
fee_schedule_agency: Virginia Workers' Compensation Commission, Medical Fee Services Department
pharmacy_basis: Prescription drugs are excluded from the VWC Medical Fee Schedules; reimbursement is at the lesser of the provider's usual and customary charge or AWP minus 10% (no dispensing fee for certain drug types); physician-dispensed medications are capped at the AWP-based schedule rate for the original NDC; generic substitution is required under Va. Code § 65.2-603.1
settlement_approval_agency_full: Virginia Workers' Compensation Commission, Judicial Division — Deputy Commissioner
settlement_approval_quirk: Compromise settlement petitions are reviewed and approved by a VWC Deputy Commissioner, who must find the settlement serves the best interests of the claimant; the petition must include a statement on the claimant's Medicare status, and approval typically takes 60 to 90 days from filing.
quirk_1: Virginia requires the employer to provide a panel of at least three independent physicians; the claimant's selection from that panel establishes the authorized treating clinician, and any change thereafter requires carrier consent or a Commission order; the authorized treating clinician's records are the primary evidentiary basis for projecting future medical care in a WCMSA.
quirk_2: Prescription drugs fall outside the VWC fee schedule and are reimbursed on an AWP-minus basis; because pharmacy costs are not capped by the same schedule as facility or professional services, WCMSA drug allocations must be calculated separately using current AWP-based pricing.
quirk_3: The VWC updates its Medical Fee Schedules on a biennial cycle; the current 2026-2027 edition controls for that two-year window, and a WCMSA prepared near the end of a schedule period should confirm which edition applies to the projected treatment dates.

fee_schedule_short: Medical Aid Rules and Fee Schedules (MARFS)
fee_schedule_full: Washington State Medical Aid Rules and Fee Schedules (MARFS), published annually by the Department of Labor and Industries under RCW Title 51
fee_schedule_agency: Washington State Department of Labor and Industries (L&I)
pharmacy_basis: L&I formulary using a subset of the Washington State Preferred Drug List (PDL) plus a wrap-around formulary; prescription drugs for initial state-fund claim visits covered regardless of formulary status; pharmacy-only schedule (does not apply to clinician-administered drugs in office)
settlement_approval_agency_full: Washington State Board of Industrial Insurance Appeals
settlement_approval_quirk: Claim Resolution Settlement Agreements (CRSAs) are drafted by L&I, circulated for signatures, then submitted to the BIIA for approval; any party may revoke consent within 30 days of approval before the settlement becomes final.
quirk_1: Washington is a monopolistic state-fund jurisdiction: L&I is the sole workers' compensation insurer for all non-self-insured employers, so all fee schedule rules, formulary decisions, and settlement processes run through a single state entity with no private carrier variation to account for in WCMSA projections.
quirk_2: CRSAs exchange all future L&I indemnity benefits for a lump sum or structured payment while leaving medical benefits open; self-insured employers use CRSAs more frequently, and the open-medical retention means the WCMSA allocation must be carefully delineated from continuing L&I medical obligations.
quirk_3: The 2025 MARFS (effective July 1 2025) include a dedicated Chapter 19 for pharmacy; L&I uses treatment guidelines and formulary status to determine drug coverage, so projected drugs in a WCMSA must be confirmed against the current formulary and L&I policy.

fee_schedule_full: West Virginia Workers' Compensation Medical Reimbursement Fee Schedule (West Virginia Medicare rates plus 35 percent), maintained under WV Code § 23-4-3
fee_schedule_agency: West Virginia Offices of the Insurance Commissioner Workers' Compensation Division
pharmacy_basis: NADAC plus a dispensing fee of $10.49; WAC plus $10.49 dispensing fee applies when NADAC is unavailable; generic substitution is mandatory under WV Code § 23-4-3 unless a brand-name is medically required, in which case the claimant bears the cost difference
settlement_approval_agency_full: West Virginia Offices of the Insurance Commissioner, Compromise Settlement Division (under the Workers' Compensation Board of Review framework effective July 1 2022)
settlement_approval_quirk: Every settlement proposal must be submitted to the Compromise Settlement Division for review; the Insurance Commissioner may void settlements with unrepresented claimants found to be unconscionable, and each agreement provides the claimant a five-business-day revocation window after execution.
quirk_1: West Virginia exited its monopolistic state-fund structure in 2006 and now operates a fully privatized competitive market; WCMSAs are prepared against the same federal CMS thresholds as other states, but the absence of a state fund means there is no single carrier-side point of contact, so the responsible carrier at settlement controls the WCMSA submission and administration process.
quirk_2: Employers who participate in an approved Managed Health Care Plan (MHCP) can require injured workers to use network providers; where an MHCP governs, future medical care projected in the WCMSA should reflect network-contracted rates, because the injured worker's post-settlement care may be channeled through the network.
quirk_3: The Office of Judges was eliminated effective July 1 2022 and the Board of Review now holds exclusive jurisdiction over initial claim objections; any pending claim disputes at settlement that affect the scope of compensable conditions must be resolved through the Board of Review process before a WCMSA allocation can be finalized on a settled liability basis.

fee_schedule_short: No fee schedule for professional services (hospital schedule pending)
fee_schedule_full: Wisconsin Worker's Compensation Medical Fee Schedule — hospital services fee schedule in rulemaking (DWD deadline July 1 2027); no professional services fee schedule in effect
fee_schedule_agency: Wisconsin Department of Workforce Development (DWD), Division of Worker's Compensation
pharmacy_basis: AWP plus $3.00 dispensing fee (generic); no specific compounded medication guidance; Wisconsin follows existing pharmacy fee schedule basis
settlement_approval_agency_full: Wisconsin Department of Workforce Development, Division of Worker's Compensation
settlement_approval_quirk: All compromise agreements must be in writing or placed on the record at a hearing and approved by the DWD Division of Worker's Compensation to confirm reasonableness; orders on compromise must be paid within 21 days.
quirk_1: Wisconsin has no medical fee schedule for professional services; reimbursement for clinician services is determined by a statistical formula under Wis. Stat. 102.16 using a DWD-certified database (fees above the mean plus 1.2 standard deviations are presumptively unreasonable), so WCMSA projections for professional services cannot be pegged to a published schedule and require UCR-based analysis.
quirk_2: A hospital fee schedule was enacted as part of the 2025 biennial budget but DWD has until July 1 2027 to complete and publish it; until that date, hospital services also remain on a UCR basis, so all medical cost projections in WCMSA reports prepared before mid-2027 must use charge data without a scheduled ceiling.
quirk_3: Wisconsin's compromise procedure requires DWD approval of reasonableness, and the written or on-record agreement is the triggering document; the absence of a formal hearing officer approval system (as in many states) means settlement timing is driven by DWD administrative review, which affects how quickly WCMSA funds can be placed and administered post-settlement.

fee_schedule_full: Wyoming Workers' Compensation Rules, Regulations and Fee Schedules, Chapter 9: Fee Schedules
fee_schedule_agency: Wyoming Department of Workforce Services, Workers' Compensation Division
pharmacy_basis: Usual and customary charge; pharmaceuticals must be billed with the original manufacturer's NDC; compounded medications are reimbursed at 130% of the pharmacy invoice; custom compounds capped at $600 with charges above subject to Division review
settlement_approval_agency_short: Wyoming DWS Workers' Compensation Division / OAH
settlement_approval_agency_full: Wyoming Department of Workforce Services, Workers' Compensation Division (with contested disputes resolved by the Wyoming Office of Administrative Hearings)
settlement_approval_quirk: Wyoming's monopolistic fund means all settlements are negotiated directly with the DWS Workers' Compensation Division; disputed claims or denials proceed to the Office of Administrative Hearings (OAH) where a hearing examiner may convene a medical hearing panel on contested clinical questions before a settlement or determination is finalized.
quirk_1: Wyoming mandates use of the most current edition of the AMA Guides to the Evaluation of Permanent Impairment at the time of ascertainable loss; the state currently applies the Sixth Edition, which uses a diagnosis-based model that differs materially from the edition-specific frameworks used in neighboring states, and impairment ratings under the Sixth Edition anchor the permanent partial impairment (PPI) benefits that feed into settlement value.
quirk_2: Wyoming's Chapter 9 professional fee schedule uses a national RBRVS framework with six separate conversion factors (by service category) and incorporates Medicare rates as the basis for ambulance (Medicare plus 10%) and DME/supplies (Medicare HCPCS plus 30%); a WCMSA uses Wyoming-specific conversion factors as the cost basis for projected services.
quirk_3: Wyoming requires prior authorization for hospitalizations, surgeries, and certain other medical care under Wyoming Statute 24-14-601(o); a WCMSA projecting future surgical or inpatient needs must account for this preauthorization requirement, because the Division's preauthorization decisions will govern whether projected care is actually payable after settlement.
caselaw_citation: VERIFY (agent attempted Anderson v. State of Wyoming citation; case number and date inconsistent — confirm before use)
